nodes_flat.title: 1. Fire and Emergency Policy
nodes_flat.summary: Our Fire & Emergency Policy prioritizes your child's safety. We hold termly emergency drills and annual Risk Assessments. Health & Safety Officers and an External Technician identify hazards. Our Leadership Team validates the Emergency Plan, ensuring clear evacuation procedures for everyone, including families, visitors, and contractors. If you're on-site during an evacuation, please follow staff instructions immediately to the Fire Assembly Point. All visitors sign in/out, wear a green lanyard, and are escorted by staff. Evacuation routes are always clear. Fire alarms are regularly tested; firefighting equipment annually. The Leadership Team reviews this policy biennially (every two years). We use Contractor Hazard Exchange Forms for outside workers.

nodes_flat.title: 1.1. Scope
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori prioritizes child and staff safety. An emergency drill is carried out every term. Risk assessments are done annually or when significant changes occur. Health and Safety Officers identify hazards, assess risks, and manage control measures. An External Technician in Emergency Risk Assessment also identifies hazards, evaluates risks, and records findings annually. These roles collaborate to create an emergency plan, validated by the Leadership Team and practiced with the whole Imagine Team.
nodes_flat.content:
The safety of the children and staff at Imagine Montessori School is paramount. Therefore, the risk assessment ensures the safety of everyone. There is an emergency drill carried out every term. A risk assessment is made annually or when there is a significant change or new risk to consider.

**The Health and Safety Officers:**

*   Identify the hazard, record significant findings and assess the risks
*   Review the existing control measures
*   Where necessary, provide additional control measures
*   Carry out risk assessments

**The External Technician in Emergency Risk Assessment is responsible for:**

*   Identification of hazards
*   Detect who might be harmed and how
*   Evaluate the risks, deciding whether existing controls are adequate, or the school needs to do more
*   Record findings
*   Review and revise annually

These responsible agents collaborate to prepare an emergency plan in accordance with the risks identified. The emergency plan is validated with the Leadership Team and explained and practised with the whole Imagine Team.

nodes_flat.title: 1.2. Terminology
nodes_flat.summary: Understanding safety terms is crucial. A Fire and Emergency Policy outlines rules for building safety during emergencies. Risk Assessment identifies and evaluates dangers. Health and Safety Officers manage hazards and oversee procedures. An External Technician is an outside expert for emergency risk evaluation. The Emergency Plan details procedures for everyone's safety. The Fire Assembly Point is the designated gathering spot after evacuation. A Contractor Hazard Exchange Form manages risks from outside workers.
nodes_flat.content:
Fire and Emergency Policy: The policy established to ensure the safety of individuals in the building in the event of a fire or other emergency.

Risk Assessment: The process of identifying and evaluating risks to ensure a safe environment.

Health and Safety Officers: Personnel responsible for identifying hazards, implementing control measures, and overseeing safety procedures.

External Technician in Emergency Risk Assessment: An external specialist responsible for evaluating emergency risks and providing expert guidance.

Emergency Plan: A document outlining the procedures to be followed during an emergency to ensure the safety of everyone on the premises.

Fire Assembly Point: A designated area where individuals must gather after evacuating the building.

Contractor Hazard Exchange Form/Checklist: A tool used to assess and mitigate specific risks associated with contractors working on the premises.

nodes_flat.title: 1.3. Policy Statement and Principles
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori is committed to safety for all (children, staff, visitors). Our policy includes continuous risk assessments to mitigate hazards, regular drills & staff training for swift emergency responses, and clear evacuation procedures shared with families, visitors, & contractors. We maintain accessible evacuation routes and optimal emergency equipment through regular inspections, ensuring a safe, orderly environment during any emergency.
nodes_flat.content:
Imagine Montessori School is committed to ensuring the safety of all children, staff, and visitors on campus. This commitment is reflected in the implementation of policies and practices that:

1.  Ensure continuous risk assessments to identify hazards and mitigate them effectively.
2.  Promote regular drills and staff training to guarantee swift and organised responses in case of emergencies.
3.  Provide clear information and evacuation procedures to all parties involved, including families, visitors, and contractors.
4.  Maintain accessible evacuation routes and ensure emergency equipment is in optimal condition through regular inspections.

These principles are designed to ensure a safe and orderly environment during any emergency.

nodes_flat.title: 1.4. Emergency Evacuation Procedures for Families, Visitors, and Volunteers
nodes_flat.summary: If you, as a parent, visitor, or volunteer, are on school premises during an emergency evacuation, the key rule is to immediately follow all instructions from the designated staff member in charge. This ensures everyone's safety during the "emergency evacuation procedure."
nodes_flat.content: In case any family, visitors or volunteers are on the school premises when an emergency evacuation takes place, please follow the instructions of the member of staff in charge.

nodes_flat.title: 1.5. Staff absences
nodes_flat.summary: During staff absences, the teacher or adult in charge of the class will undertake duties for evacuating children. Supply staff are also required to sweep any areas they pass through for personnel as they exit the building during an evacuation.
nodes_flat.content: Should any member of staff be absent, their duties in evacuating the children in their care from the building will be undertaken by the teacher or adult in charge of the class. Supply staff will also be required to sweep any areas they pass through for personnel as they exit the building.

nodes_flat.title: 1.6. Visitor Registration and Safety Procedures
nodes_flat.summary: All visitors and contractors must sign in at Reception, wear a green lanyard, and sign out upon leaving. During a fire evacuation, host staff escort visitors to the fire assembly point. Recurrent contractors (e.g., cleaning) have adapted fire protocols and training, covering evacuation, assembly points, and equipment. Non-recurrent contractors receive a brief on emergency exits and assembly points upon arrival, joining an evacuation team if needed. This ensures everyone's safety on premises.
nodes_flat.content:
**Visitor Registration**

All visitors and contractors must report to Reception, signing in upon arrival and signing out before leaving the premises. Once signed in, they will receive a green lanyard, which must be worn throughout their visit. During a fire evacuation, the staff member hosting the visitor is responsible for ensuring they are escorted to the designated fire assembly point.

**Contractors Safety Procedures**

For contractors, there is a distinction between recurrent and non-recurrent roles:

*   **Recurrent contractors (e.g., cleaning or kitchen staff)** have their adapted Fire and Emergency Protocol, and receive basic fire safety training to ensure they are prepared for emergencies, even outside regular school hours. They are informed of the action to be taken on hearing the fire alarm or discovering a fire, the evacuation procedures including means of escape, location of the fire assembly points and name of the person in charge of evacuation procedures, and the location of firefighting equipment and fire alarm call points in relation to the area of their work.
*   **Non-recurrent contractors** receive a brief summary of emergency procedures upon arrival, including the location of emergency exits and the fire assembly point. In the event of an evacuation, they are instructed to join the nearest evacuation team and follow their guidance to ensure a safe exit.

This approach ensures all individuals on the premises are informed and capable of responding appropriately in an emergency.

nodes_flat.title: 1.7. Evacuation routes
nodes_flat.summary: Emergency evacuation routes will always be kept clear of any obstructions. These critical pathways will also be adequately and clearly marked, ensuring everyone can easily find their way out safely during an emergency.
nodes_flat.content: Evacuation routes will be kept free from obstruction and adequately and clearly marked.

nodes_flat.title: Fire Safety Equipment Testing
nodes_flat.summary: For safety, fire alarms are regularly tested by a certified agent. All firefighting equipment, like extinguishers, is examined and tested at least once a year by a competent service engineer. This ensures all gear is ready and compliant.
nodes_flat.content:
The fire alarms are regularly tested by a certified agent.

Firefighting equipment will be examined and tested at least once a year by a competent service engineer.

nodes_flat.title: 1.10. Monitoring and Review
nodes_flat.summary: Who keeps an eye on this policy? The Imagine Leadership Team. They're in charge of monitoring and reviewing it. This happens biennially, so you can expect it to be checked and updated every two years.
nodes_flat.content: The Imagine Leadership Team is responsible for monitoring and reviewing the policy biennially.

nodes_flat.title: 1.11. Reference Documents
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori School's safety protocols are guided by Workplace Health and Safety Regulations, a Risk Assessment and Emergency Planning Guide for Educational Institutions, and Fire Evacuation and Prevention Regulations from the Department for Education and Safety. We manage contractor safety using Hazard Exchange Forms and Checklists. Our Imagine Montessori School Emergency Procedures Protocol details all school-specific emergency responses, ensuring student well-being.
nodes_flat.content:
*   Workplace Health and Safety Regulations.
*   Risk Assessment and Emergency Planning Guide for Educational Institutions.
*   Fire Evacuation and Prevention Regulations from the Department for Education and Safety.
*   Contractor Hazard Exchange Forms and Checklists.
*   Imagine Montessori School Emergency Procedures Protocol.

nodes_flat.title: 2. Health & Safety Policy
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori's H&S policy ensures a safe environment, adhering to Law 31/1995 and Decreto 253/2019. Responsibility is shared: HR (Workplace Safety), Facility Manager (Buildings), Head Teachers/Admin (daily measures). Key terms: Risk Assessment (identifying hazards), First Aider (trained staff). Maintenance performs daily safety checks; the policy is reviewed biennially. All staff receive H&S training (risk assessment, evacuation, first-aid) and are qualified first-aiders, trained annually, exceeding ratios. The Secretary handles minor accidents; trained first-aiders act for emergencies. Parents are notified via Phidias and sign accident reports. Medication is administered with signed consent, witnessed, and recorded. A wasp sting protocol details actions for mild vs. severe reactions (anaphylaxis, call 112, EpiPen if prescribed). Breaches are documented via a 'Record of Concern.' The school upholds a 'duty of care' like a 'prudent parent.'

nodes_flat.title: 2.1. Scope
nodes_flat.summary: Children engage in varied activities, learning risk management. Our health & safety (H&S) procedures are proportionate. The school, as employer, must have an H&S policy (Law 31/1995, Prevention of Occupational Risks), assess/manage risks, and communicate measures. Preventive activities follow Royal Decree 39/1997. While H&S tasks can be delegated, ultimate responsibility always rests with the employer, requiring clear documentation and an audit trail. At Imagine Montessori School, H&S is shared: the HR Responsible handles Workplace Safety, the Facility Manager oversees Buildings/Facilities, and Head Teachers/Head of Administration implement practical H&S measures daily.
nodes_flat.content:
Children should have the opportunity to engage in a broad variety of activities. Health and safety measures should support this goal, ensuring their well-being without hindering their participation. It is crucial for children to learn to recognise and manage risks as part of their everyday life. When assessing and managing risks, common sense should be applied, and health and safety procedures should always be proportionate to the risks associated with each activity. Staff should receive appropriate training to ensure they can protect themselves and the children, and effectively manage any potential risks.

**Responsibility of the school**

The employer is required to have a health and safety policy in place and ensure the necessary measures are implemented to enforce it. This is regulated under the Law 31/1995 on the *Prevention of Occupational Risks*. Employers are obligated to assess the risks associated with all activities, adopt measures to manage those risks, and communicate these measures to their employees. Furthermore, the organisation of preventive activities is governed by the Regulation of Prevention Services (*Royal Decree 39/1997*).

In practice, employers may delegate specific health and safety tasks to designated individuals. However, ultimate responsibility always remains with the employer, regardless of who carries out the tasks.

It is therefore essential for the employer to maintain a clear system of documentation and monitoring to ensure there is an audit trail. This should specify who is responsible for each task and confirm that these tasks are being performed appropriately.

**Responsibility of the employees**

At Imagine Montessori School, responsibility for health and safety is shared across different roles. To manage health and safety effectively, the following responsibilities are defined:

*   **Policy Definition and Oversight:** Specific areas are assigned to designated individuals, such as the Responsibility for Workplace Safety (HR Responsible) and oversight of Buildings and Facilities (Facility Manager).
*   **Implementation:** The practical application of health and safety measures is managed by the Head Teachers and the Head of Administration within their respective domains.

This collaborative structure ensures that all areas of the school’s operations maintain the highest standards of safety and compliance.

nodes_flat.title: 2.2. Terminology
nodes_flat.summary: Key terms include Risk Assessment, a process to identify hazards, evaluate risks, and implement controls. A First Aider is a trained, certified staff member providing immediate medical assistance.
nodes_flat.content:
Risk Assessment: The process of identifying hazards, evaluating risks, and implementing measures to control them.

First Aider: A staff member trained and certified to provide immediate medical assistance.

nodes_flat.title: 2.3. Policy Statement and Principles
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori commits to a safe, healthy environment for all. Our Maintenance Team performs daily safety checks (indoors/outdoors) for damage (e.g., fallen tiles), hazards (glass, animal faeces, dangerous plants), clear entrances/exits, and unlocked emergency exits. Health and safety measures are reviewed annually; electrical/gas equipment is inspected, with service histories logged, and strict hygiene maintained. All staff receive H&S training, covering risk assessment, emergency evacuation, first-aid, and awareness of illness signs like meningitis, hepatitis B, AIDS. Maintenance staff are trained in utility shut-offs. Contractors are vetted for H&S awareness and track record. The main door is always locked; two doors are supervised for children's arrival/departure. Slips/trips are prevented, with Head Teachers conducting periodic risk assessments. All accidents, including RIDDOR 1995 reportable ones, are recorded. Fire safety, off-site visits, and staff violence are detailed in separate policies.

nodes_flat.title: General Policy Statement
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori's policy is a firm commitment to a safe and healthy environment for all. This includes our employees, your children, and every visitor on site.
nodes_flat.content:
**A. A general statement of policy**

Imagine Montessori is committed to providing a safe and healthy work environment for all its employees, children and visitors.

**

nodes_flat.title: Daily Safety Checks and Responsibilities
nodes_flat.summary: Daily safety checks are performed by the Maintenance Team. They inspect the building (indoors/outdoors) for damage like fallen tiles, notifying the Facilities Manager immediately and making areas safe. They clear hazards (glass, litter, animal faeces). Checks include clear entrances/exits, unlocked emergency exits, safe climbing equipment, and garden hazards (fungi, dangerous plants). Emergency lighting and general illumination are also checked. All staff uphold safety duties.
nodes_flat.content:
B. Delegation of duties as allocated tasks**

*   The general safety of the building, indoors and outdoors, is checked daily by the Maintenance Team. This includes checking the general condition of the building, e.g. fallen tiles. In finding any damage, the Facilities Manager of the school will be notified immediately. The area will be made safe. If there is any broken glass, litter, cans, ring pulls as well as animal faeces all should be cleared.
*   Check all entrances and exits are clear and in working order.
*   Unlock and check all emergency exits, garden fences and gates, locks and bolts on doors.
*   Check that all climbing equipment is safe. Check the garden for fungi and unwanted dangerous plants.
*   Check that all emergency lighting is in working order, that light switches are safe, and that all areas are adequately lit.
*   Other duties all members of staff are expected to be aware of and uphold.

**

nodes_flat.title: Monitoring and Review of Health and Safety Measures
nodes_flat.summary: Health and safety measures are reviewed annually. All electrical and gas equipment is maintained and periodically inspected, with service histories recorded in a logbook. Faults and repairs are logged; damaged items are labelled, removed, and then repaired or discarded. Strict hygiene standards are always maintained.
nodes_flat.content:
C. Arrangements made to put in place, monitor and review measures necessary to reach satisfactory health and safety standards:**

All arrangements are monitored and reviewed annually. All electrical and gas equipment is to be maintained and subject to periodical inspections. The service histories should be recorded in a logbook.

Faults and repairs are to be logged. For example, a piece of equipment that is damaged should be labelled and removed until it can be either repaired or discarded. Strict hygiene standards are set and maintained.

**

nodes_flat.title: Staff Training in Health and Safety
nodes_flat.summary: All staff receive health & safety training, including risk assessment competence, during induction and a rolling program. This covers school hygiene, emergency evacuation, and first-aid. Staff are also made aware of illness signs like meningitis, hepatitis B, AIDS. Maintenance staff are trained in utility shut-offs and safe electrical equipment use. Regular meetings address H&S, e.g., tetanus vaccinations. Off-site visits are detailed in a separate Trips and Outings Policy.
nodes_flat.content:
D. Training of staff in health and safety including competence in risk assessment**

All members of staff should have the school Health and Safety procedures clearly explained to them as part of the induction process and are given the Health and Safety policies in their induction pack. This includes the school hygiene regime. As part of a rolling programme, staff are trained in Health and Safety including competence in risk assessment.

All new staff should be trained in the evacuation procedures in the event of a fire or other emergency. All members of staff should have first-aid training (see first-aid policy). All staff are to be kept aware of signs of illness such as meningitis, hepatitis B, AIDS. All maintenance staff must know where to turn the water and electricity supplies off and must be trained to use electrical equipment correctly and safely. Use regular staff meetings to focus on different health and safety issues such as keeping up to date on tetanus vaccinations.

**E. Off-site visits including school-led adventure activities**

See separate policy: Trips and Outings Policy

**

nodes_flat.title: Contractor Selection and Control
nodes_flat.summary: To select and manage contractors, the school checks their track record, experience with other schools, and if they belong to accredited trade bodies. Crucially, we assess their health and safety awareness, safety arrangements, and commitment to industry codes. This ensures qualified, safe professionals work on school premises.
nodes_flat.content:
F. Selecting and controlling contractors**

The school will use these indicators to select and control contractors: past performance, work undertaken in other schools, membership of trade bodies, accreditation of trade bodies, general health and safety awareness, organisation and arrangements for health and safety and commitment to recognised codes of practice.

**

nodes_flat.title: Workplace Safety and Emergency Procedures
nodes_flat.summary: School security involves daily Maintenance Team checks. The main door is always locked. Two doors are continuously supervised by Imagine’s staff for children's arrival/departure; other doors stay secure. Workplace safety for children, staff, and visitors is ensured by school regulations. Staff are consulted on health/safety. Workplace stress is managed via risk assessments. Slips/trips are prevented by clearing spills, removing obstructions, and wearing sensible shoes; Head Teachers conduct periodic risk assessments. Violence to staff is covered in the Behaviour Policy (Document "5. Code of Conduct").
nodes_flat.content:
H. School Security**

This is covered in the check of the inside and external building by the Maintenance Team. The main door is always locked. The doors that are used to access the outside areas are only opened when such areas are under supervision. At the start and end of the school day, two doors are used for children to arrive or depart, and both are continuously supervised by Imagine’s staff. All other doors remain secure and are not used during these times.

**I. Occupational health services and work-related stress**

The school will identify all workplace stressors and conduct risk assessments to eliminate stress or control the risks from stress. These risk assessments will be regularly reviewed.

**J. Consultation arrangements with staff**

The school shall consult its employees in good time on matters relating to their health and safety at work regarding the introduction of any measure in the school that will affect the health and safety of the employees; any health and safety information they are required to provide to those employees by or under the relevant statutory provisions.

**K. Workplace safety for teachers, pupils and visitors**

The school through all its health and safety regulations ensures the workplace is safe for all.

**L. Violence to staff**

See separate policy: Behaviour Policy (Document “5. Code of Conduct”)

**M. Slips and trips:**

Everyone in the school has a part to play when it comes to preventing slips and trips: clear spillages immediately, remove any obstructions and wear sensible shoes. Periodically, the Head Teachers develop risk assessments.

**N. On site vehicle movements:**

Not applicable

**

nodes_flat.title: Accident Reporting and Maintenance
nodes_flat.summary: The school maintains safety by using external contractors for regular examination and testing of equipment like electrical systems, gas appliances, and lifting gear, addressing identified hazards. All accidents involving staff, pupils, and visitors are recorded and reported, including those reportable under RIDDOR 1995. Fire safety, including alarm testing and evacuation procedures, is detailed in a separate Fire Safety Policy. Health and safety emergencies are managed through established policies, guidance, and training.
nodes_flat.content:
O. Maintenance and when necessary, examination and test of plant and equipment such as electrical equipment, local exhaust ventilation, pressure systems, gas appliances, lifting equipment and glazing safety**

The school would identify the hazard or suspected hazard and use an external contractor.

**P. Recording and reporting accidents to staff, pupils and visitors - including those reportable under the Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations 1995 (RIDDOR).**

The school would record any of the above.

**Q. Fire safety, including testing of alarms and evacuation procedures**

See separate policy: Fire Safety Policy

**R. Dealing with health and safety emergencies- procedures and contacts**

By having policies, guidance and training for all the above, the school can deal with any emergencies using the correct procedures and contacts.

nodes_flat.title: 2.4. First aid
nodes_flat.summary: All Imagine staff are qualified first-aiders, trained annually, well above the required ratio. For minor accidents, the Secretary is the designated staff member. For medical emergencies, trained first-aiders act immediately. First aid equipment is in the designated cupboard in the admin staff room and bathroom. All accidents are recorded on an accident report form. Parents/guardians are notified (Phidias for bus service) and asked to sign the record, which the Secretary keeps (paper/digital). Risk analyses are periodically conducted using multivariable analysis to mitigate recurring risks. If an accident is serious, the Head Teacher is informed. Staff are aware of pupils' medical conditions (e.g., epilepsy, asthma, severe allergies). The Secretary administers prescribed medication with a signed parent/guardian consent form, witnessed by another staff member, with a written record kept. Staff know what injury levels trigger an emergency ambulance call or hospital visit. Hygiene for blood/body fluid spills involves disposable gloves/aprons, cleaning with hot water/soap then bleac
nodes_flat.content:
All Imagine’s staff have qualified first-aider status and are trained on an annual recurrent basis. Therefore, we have well above the required ratio of first aiders to pupils. For minor accidents, they will refer any accidents or any other medical conditions to the designated member of staff: Secretary. For medical emergencies, the person who finds the child/adult will deal with it immediately. Only trained first aiders administer first aid.

There is always more than one designated first-aider on the site of the school. All first aiders attend training courses biannually. First aid equipment is in the designated first aid cupboard, which is kept in the admin staff room and bathroom.

All accidents and other medical emergencies should be recorded appropriately in an accident report form. Parents /guardians should be notified and asked to sign the record sheet and a record kept. In the case of students using our bus service, parents will be notified by Phidias and we expect an acknowledgment. The accident report will be kept both on paper and in digital format at the Secretary.

Based on the information from accident reports, risk analyses are periodically conducted using multivariable analysis to implement mitigation actions for recurring potential risks.

If the accident is serious, the head teacher should be made aware and immediate action taken to ensure the location of the accident is still safe to use. Necessary action should be taken and where possible details recorded for an accident investigation. If members of the public are involved, names and addresses should be taken (including any witnesses).

Members of staff should be made aware of any hazards in the subjects that they teach or the activities that they supervise and should have appropriate training and access to first aid kits.

Pupils with medical conditions, such as epilepsy, asthma or severe allergies should be identified. All staff should be aware of their conditions and alert to the need for prompt action. The Secretary administers medication and is witnessed by another member of staff. A written record is kept and signed by the two members of staff. Medication is kept in the office or the refrigerator in Reception. A signed consent form is needed from the parent /guardian before prescribed medication is administered.

Staff should be aware of what levels of injury can be treated on-site and what levels automatically trigger an emergency ambulance call or visit to the hospital.

There are hygiene procedures in place in case of spillage of blood or body fluid: Employees must wear disposable gloves and aprons when cleaning any bodily fluids (spillages of blood, vomit, urine and excreta). Other persons should be kept away from the contamination until it is effectively dealt with.

The following action should be taken in the event of spillages:

*   Soak up the spillages using paper towels
*   Clean the area with hot water containing soap or detergent
*   Clean the area with a bleach or disinfectant solution - use as directed by the manufacturer.
*   'Solids’ should be carefully flushed down the toilet. Dressings, gloves, and aprons should be carefully disposed of in sealed plastic bags as clinical waste.

nodes_flat.title: 2.5. Protocol for action in case of wasp stings in students
nodes_flat.summary: Wasp sting protocol: Move student from danger, check for more wasps. Assess severity: single/multiple stings, known allergy? Mild reactions (pain, redness, localized swelling) require sting removal, washing, cold compress (10-15 min), elevation, and 30 min observation. For severe reactions (anaphylaxis: breathing difficulty, face/neck swelling, widespread hives, dizziness, vomiting), call 112 immediately, administer self-injectable adrenaline (EpiPen) if available/prescribed, and position the child with legs elevated. Do not leave them. Notify family immediately, record incident, and monitor for 1 hour. Prevention: check premises, staff training, first aid kit with cold packs. EpiPen use: remove cap, press against outer thigh for 5-10 seconds. Always call 112 and notify family after EpiPen use; hospital visit is essential. School kit includes cold packs, prescribed antihistamines, and allergy lists.
nodes_flat.content:
**1. Initial assessment and safety**

*   Immediately move the student away from the area where the wasps are present to prevent further stings.
*   Check for additional wasps nearby before intervening.

**2. Assess severity**

*   Ask and observe:
    *   Single sting or multiple stings?
    *   Are you known to be allergic to insect stings?
    *   Initial symptoms:
        *   Mild: pain, redness, localised swelling.
        *   Emergency: difficulty breathing, swelling of the face/neck, widespread hives, dizziness, vomiting.

**3. Immediate action according to the situation**

**A. Mild local reaction (most common)**

*   Remove the sting if present (wasps usually do not leave one, but check).
*   Wash the area with soap and water.
*   Apply a cold compress (ice wrapped in a cloth) for 10–15 minutes to reduce swelling and pain.
*   Elevate the affected area if possible (e.g. an arm).
*   Observe the student for at least 30 minutes to rule out delayed reactions.

**B. Severe reaction (anaphylaxis) – EMERGENCY**

Warning symptoms:

*   Swelling of the lips, tongue, or throat.
*   Shortness of breath or a feeling of tightness in the chest.
*   Severe dizziness or loss of consciousness.
*   Generalised urticaria.
*   Persistent vomiting.

**Action:**

*   Call 112 immediately (state: *“possible anaphylactic reaction due to sting”*).
*   Administer self-injectable adrenaline (if reported by the family and available at the school).
*   Place the child lying down with legs elevated (if conscious).
*   Do not leave the child unattended until medical assistance arrives.

**4. Information and monitoring**

*   Notify the family immediately.
*   Record the incident (time, place, symptoms, and actions taken).
*   Monitor the student for at least 1 hour, even in mild cases, as delayed reactions may occur.

**5. Prevention**

*   Check the premises before use.
*   Inform staff to act quickly but calmly.
*   Ensure a first aid kit with instant cold packs and basic materials is available.
*   Keep a list of students with known allergies and the location of their auto-injectors.

**6. Training of staff in the use of an EpiPen**

**How to use the EpiPen:**

1.  Remove the safety cap.
2.  Hold firmly and place against the outer thigh (it can be applied over clothing).
3.  Press until you hear a click, hold for 5–10 seconds.
4.  Massage the area gently.

*   After use: call 112 and notify the family.
*   Ensure the child is taken to hospital, even if symptoms improve.
*   School Emergency Kit:
    *   Instant cold packs (for mild stings).
    *   Oral antihistamine (only if prescribed for the student).
    *   Gauze, gloves, disinfectant.
    *   List of emergency telephone numbers (112, family contacts).
    *   List of students with allergies and the location of auto-injectors (if applicable).

nodes_flat.title: 2.6. Monitoring and Review
nodes_flat.summary: The Imagine Leadership Team is responsible for monitoring and reviewing this policy every two years (biennially). Any policy breaches are formally documented using a "Record of Concern" form. This process ensures accountability and provides a clear record for addressing any issues or non-compliance.
nodes_flat.content: School Leadership: The Imagine Leadership Team is responsible for monitoring and reviewing the policy biennially, with all instances of policy breaches documented through a “Record of Concern” form as needed.

nodes_flat.title: 2.7. Reference Documents
nodes_flat.summary: Valencian schools follow strict health and safety rules. Decreto 253/2019 (DOGV) ensures comprehensive safety for Early Childhood/Primary, covering infrastructure, emergency plans, and action protocols. Ley 10/2014 and Ley 1/1994 mandate health promotion and disease prevention programs for students. The Ley 31/1995 (Occupational Risk Prevention Act) manages risks. School staff have a "duty of care" like a "prudent parent"; responsibility for accidents is typically civil, not criminal.
nodes_flat.content:
The main legislation covering health and safety in educational settings in the Valencian Community includes the Decreto 253/2019, de 29 de noviembre (DOGV), which regulates the organisation and functioning of public centres providing Early Childhood and Primary Education. This decree establishes guidelines for comprehensive safety within schools, covering aspects related to infrastructure, emergency measures, and protocols for action.

The Ley 10/2014, de 29 de diciembre, de Salud de la Comunitat Valenciana, specifies that the competent authorities, in collaboration with the educational and child protection departments, will develop educational and training programmes to promote healthy habits and prevent diseases in the school environment.

The Ley 1/1994, de 28 de marzo, de Salud Escolar, also establishes actions related to health in schools, with a strong emphasis on promoting health and preventing diseases among the student population.

In addition, the Ley 31/1995 (Occupational Risk Prevention Act) and the Reglamento de los Servicios de Prevención (Regulation of Prevention Services) apply to the educational environment, ensuring that risks are identified, managed, and mitigated. Employees also have a duty to ensure their own and others' health and safety. It is rare for school staff to face prosecution under criminal law regarding accidents involving children, as responsibility is generally considered under civil law, with a standard of care similar to that of a prudent parent.

nodes_flat.title: 3. Policy of action in the event of weather alerts and emergencies
nodes_flat.summary:
The school's policy ensures child/staff/family safety during weather alerts, using GVA/AEMET risk levels.
**Green:** Normal.
**Yellow (Moderate Risk/GVA Level 0):** Outdoor activities suspended, premises inspected, families informed.
**Orange (Significant Risk/GVA Level 1):** Outdoor activities suspended, early closure considered, liaison with town council/Civil Protection, caution advised for travel.
**Red (Extreme Risk/GVA Level 2):** Possible centre closure by Management/authorities. If during school, evacuation/confinement evaluated. Families informed of closures/measures.
Closure decisions follow GVA/local authorities (112cv.gva.es, AEMET). School can proactively close if risk is evident. Online learning *may* be offered if classes are suspended due to orange/red alerts.
Preventive measures include generator checks, cleaning. Alerts are monitored via 112cv.gva.es, AEMET. The Head of Emergency Plan coordinates actions.
**DANA events:** If your town is in Situation 2 (emergency), children should not attend school, even if the weather alert isn't yet orange. Situation 2 typically corresponds to an Orange Alert.

nodes_flat.title: 3.1. Objective
nodes_flat.summary: This safety plan guarantees the well-being of your children, staff, and families during weather alerts. It establishes an action protocol, based on risk levels determined by the Generalitat Valenciana and AEMET, to ensure everyone's protection.
nodes_flat.content: To guarantee the safety of students, teaching and non-teaching staff, and families in weather alert situations, by establishing an action protocol in line with the level of risk determined by the Generalitat Valenciana, AEMET and the competent authorities.

nodes_flat.title: 3.2. Alert levels and action measures
nodes_flat.summary:
Our centre uses four meteorological alert levels:
**Green:** No meteorological risk, normal activity.
**Yellow:** Moderate risk. All outdoor activities (PE, excursions) are suspended. Premises are visually inspected. Families are informed of measures taken.
**Orange:** Significant risk. Outdoor activities are suspended, premises inspected. Early closure is considered. We liaise with town council/Civil Protection on access roads. Staff/families in affected areas are urged to take caution when travelling.
**Red:** Extreme risk. Centre closure (total/partial) by Management/authorities is possible. School transport is coordinated. If an alert is declared during school hours, the evacuation or confinement plan is evaluated/activated. Continuous contact with town council/Civil Protection. Families are informed of any closures or measures taken to guarantee safety.
nodes_flat.content:
| Level   | Meaning                                                               |
| :------ | :-------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Green** | There is no meteorological risk.                                      |
| **Yellow**| There is some meteorological risk for certain specific activities, but not for the population in general. |
| **Orange**| There is a significant meteorological risk. Unusual weather phenomena with a certain degree of danger for normal activities. |
| **Red**   | The meteorological risk is extreme. Unusual weather phenomena of exceptional intensity, posing a very high risk for the population. |

**GREEN LEVEL: NO RISK**

*   No special measures are taken.
*   The normal activity of the centre is maintained.

**YELLOW LEVEL: MODERATE RISK**

*   Activities at the centre:
    *   All outdoor activities (physical education, outdoor play, excursions) are suspended.
    *   A visual inspection of the premises is carried out to detect possible risks (pipes, drains, loose structures, etc.).
*   Monitoring and communication:
    *   The alert is monitored regularly through official emergency channels.
    *   Families are informed of the measures taken.

**ORANGE LEVEL: SIGNIFICANT RISK**

*   Activities at the centre:
    *   All outdoor activities (physical education, outdoor play, excursions) are suspended.
    *   A visual inspection of the premises is carried out to detect possible risks (pipes, drains, loose structures, etc.).
*   Monitoring and communication:
    *   The alert is monitored regularly through official emergency channels.
    *   Families are informed of the measures taken.
*   Transport and accessibility:
    *   Liaise with the town council or Civil Protection to assess the condition of the access roads to the centre.
    *   Early closure of the centre will be considered if conditions require it.
    *   If staff or families live in areas affected by the alert, they are urged to take extreme care when travelling and to notify the school of possible delays.

**RED LEVEL: EXTREME RISK**

*   Closure criteria:
    *   The Management may determine the total or partial closure of the educational centre depending on the severity of the alert and in accordance with the indications of the competent authorities (town council and/or Civil Protection).
*   Transport, accessibility and evacuation:
    *   Coordination with those responsible for school transport to ensure safe management.
    *   If the alert is declared during school hours, the evacuation or confinement plan due to weather risk will be evaluated and activated if necessary.
*   Monitoring and communication:
    *   Continuous contact with the town council and/or Civil Protection.
    *   In the event of preventive closure, staff and families will be informed not to attend.
    *   If the alert is declared while the centre is in operation, the necessary measures will be taken and communicated to guarantee everyone’s safety.

nodes_flat.title: Coordination, Suspension of Classes, and Educational Continuity
nodes_flat.summary: School closure decisions follow GVA/local authorities, consulting GVA emergency site (112cv.gva.es), AEMET, Civil Protection. GVA emergency levels 0, 1, 2 prompt safety reviews. If GVA/council orders closure, classes are suspended; families/staff informed; transport coordinated. If no order but evident risk, school may close proactively, informing families. During school, early dismissal or confinement is possible. For orange/red alerts outside hours, GVA/Civil Protection guidance is followed; if classes suspended, online learning *may* be offered.
nodes_flat.content:
*   The instructions of the GVA and local authorities will be followed regarding any decision on the closure of the centre.
*   The emergency website of the Generalitat Valenciana ([https://www.112cv.gva.es/es/1-1-2-on-line](https://www.112cv.gva.es/es/1-1-2-on-line)), the AEMET website and the indications of Civil Protection will be consulted to adapt the necessary measures.
*   In the event of an emergency declared by the GVA at level 0, 1 or 2, the school will assess additional measures to ensure safety.

**If the Generalitat Valenciana (GVA) or the town council orders the closure:**

*   Classes must be suspended.
*   The school will inform families and staff about the suspension of activities.
*   Coordination will be carried out with school transport and auxiliary services.

**If there is no closure order, but an evident risk exists:**

*   The school may decide to close as a precautionary measure.
*   The decision must be communicated to families in advance.
*   If the alert is activated during the school day, dismissal may be brought forward or confinement applied if access to the outside is unsafe.

**If an orange or red alert is expected outside school hours:**

*   The indications of Civil Protection and the GVA will be followed to evaluate the situation.
*   If classes are suspended, families will be informed and supported regarding the possibility of offering online educational activities if circumstances allow.

nodes_flat.title: 3.5. General preventive measures
nodes_flat.summary: To minimize risks from bad weather, periodic preventive measures are taken. These include regularly checking generator sets, cleaning pipes, gutters, and drains, verifying first aid kits, and switching off unnecessary electrical equipment during storms.
nodes_flat.content:
To minimise the risks associated with adverse weather conditions, the following preventive measures are carried out periodically:

*   Periodic review of the condition of generator sets.
*   Cleaning and maintenance of pipes, gutters and drains.
*   Verification of first aid kits and materials.
*   Switching off unnecessary electrical equipment during storms.

nodes_flat.title: 3.6. Query and monitoring of alerts
nodes_flat.summary: Alerts are queried and monitored using official sources. These include the 112CV emergency website (112cv.gva.es), the AEMET weather site, and other official channels. This process ensures all important warnings are tracked.
nodes_flat.content: Alerts will be checked through the official emergency website: [https://www.112cv.gva.es/es/1-1-2-on-line](https://www.112cv.gva.es/es/1-1-2-on-line), the AEMET website, and other official sources.

nodes_flat.title: 3.7. Responsibility and coordination
nodes_flat.summary: The Head of the Emergency Plan is responsible for overseeing all safety measures. They will coordinate with Civil Protection and the town council to adapt actions as the risk evolves. This protocol will be regularly updated based on current regulations and advice from meteorological and civil protection authorities.
nodes_flat.content:
*   The Head of the Emergency Plan in risk situations will be responsible for supervising the implementation of these measures.
*   Contact will be maintained with Civil Protection and the town council to adapt actions according to the evolution of the risk.

This protocol will be updated in line with current regulations and the recommendations of meteorological and civil protection authorities.

nodes_flat.title: 3.8. Correspondence table
nodes_flat.summary:
The school's emergency plan links GVA Emergency Levels to AEMET Alerts, guiding actions.

**Pre-emergency (Level 0 / Yellow Alert)**: Moderate risk. Schools suspend outdoor activities, inspect facilities, track alerts, and inform families.

**Emergency Level 1 (Orange Alert)**: Significant risk. Schools continue Level 0 measures, contact local authorities/Civil Protection, may consider early closure if access is affected, and advise extreme caution for families/staff in risk areas.

**Emergency Level 2 (Alert of the Day)**: Extreme risk. Schools await a closure order from authorities (GVA) or may close proactively. Immediate family communication regarding class suspension is vital, with school transport coordinated. If the alert activates during school hours, confinement or safe evacuation is evaluated.
nodes_flat.content:
| Emergency Level (GVA) | Alert Level (AEMET) | Situation | Measures for the school | | :-------------------- | :------------------ | :------------------------------------------------ | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | Pre-emergency (Level 0) | Yellow Alert | Moderate risk for certain activities, but not for the general population. | - Suspension of outdoor activities.
- Inspection of the installation.
- Alert tracking.
- Information for families. | | Emergency Level 1 | Orange Alert | Significant risk with possible impact on normal activity. | - Suspension of outdoor activities.
- Inspection of the installation.
- Alert tracking.
- Contact with local authorities and Civil Protection.
- Early closure of the centre is considered if access is affected.
- It is recommended to take extreme precautions when families or staff who reside in risk areas. | | Emergency Level 2 | Alert of the Day | Extreme risk with a very high impact on the population. | - A closure order is expected from local authorities or GVA.
- If there is no official order, the school may decide to close as a precaution.
- Immediate communication to families about the suspension of classes.
- Coordination with school transportation.
- If the alert is activated with the school open, confinement or safe evacuation is evaluated. |

nodes_flat.title: 3.9. Additional measures in case of dana events
nodes_flat.summary: For DANA events: If your town is in Situation 2 (emergency), children should not attend school, even if the weather alert isn't yet orange. Normally, Situation 1 is a yellow alert, and Situation 2 is an orange alert. This special measure prioritizes the safety of the educational community. Your cooperation and prudence are valued.
nodes_flat.content:
Under normal conditions, Situation 1 (pre-emergency) corresponds to a yellow alert and Situation 2 (emergency) to an orange alert. However, we wish to make a special recommendation for towns affected by DANA events.

If your town is in Situation 2 due to DANA, we recommend that you do not attend school, even if the weather alert has not yet reached orange level.

Our priority is the safety of the entire educational community, and we thank you for your cooperation and prudence in these cases.

nodes_flat.title: 4. Food Policy
nodes_flat.summary:
Imagine Montessori's Food Policy covers all students/staff, all meals/snacks, aligning with Montessori values (nutritious, mindful, environmental). Key terms include "Nutritive Food" (balanced for development), "Organic Products" (no synthetics/GMOs), "Seasonal Products" (fresh, in-season), "Km 0" (locally sourced), and "Special Diets" (health-based with allergist/gastroenterologist certificate, or personal like vegetarian/vegan).

We offer 3 daily meals (morning snack, lunch, afternoon snack) that are whole, unprocessed, organic, seasonal, Km 0, free of fried products, low in salt, and without added sugars. A minimum of one vegetarian day per week is provided. Students learn "Practical Life" skills by participating in meal setup/cleanup. Menus are reviewed monthly by the team and a nutritional specialist.

Religious fasting is supported; families must submit a formal request via the Phidias process to the Secretary. Fasting is generally for 12-15 year olds. Quiet spaces (e.g., science lab) are provided. Health is paramount: if a student is unwell, the school provides immediate care before contacting parents. Fasting students should avoid strenuous activity.

Policy effectiveness is monitored via feedback and an annual "Biannual Review" process by leadership. Our policy is guided by Valencian Community laws (e.g., Decreto 253/2019, Ley 10/2014 Art. 56).

nodes_flat.title: 4.1. Scope
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori School's food policy applies to all students and staff, covering all meals, snacks, and dietary considerations. It aligns with our Montessori philosophy, emphasizing nutritious food, mindful preparation, serving, and a cultural approach. This policy also supports the school's environmental values.
nodes_flat.content: This policy applies to all students and staff at Imagine Montessori School, covering all aspects of food provision, including meals, snacks, and dietary considerations. In alignment with our Montessori philosophy, it encompasses the preparation, serving, and cultural approach to food, ensuring that all meals are nutritious and supportive of the environmental values upheld by the school.

nodes_flat.title: 4.2. Terminology
nodes_flat.summary: We define key food terms: "Nutritive Food" is balanced for physical, cognitive, and emotional development. "Organic Products" are grown without synthetic pesticides, fertilizers, or GMOs, following organic standards. "Seasonal Products" are fresh, in-season, minimizing environmental impact. "Km 0" means locally sourced, reducing transport. We accommodate "Special Diets" for health (e.g., gluten-free, specific food allergies with a certificate) or personal reasons (vegetarian, vegan).
nodes_flat.content:
**Nutritive Food:** Food that is balanced, providing essential nutrients to support physical, cognitive, and emotional development.

**Organic Products:** Food grown without synthetic pesticides, fertilizers, or genetically modified organisms (GMO), in accordance with organic farming standards.

**Seasonal Products:** Ingredients that are grown or harvested at the peak of their natural season, ensuring freshness and minimal environmental impact.

**Km 0:** Food sourced locally, minimizing transportation costs and environmental footprint.

**Special Diets:** We follow any special diet due to health (gluten-free, specific food allergies, with a certificate from allergologists or gastroenterologists), or personal (vegetarian, vegan) reasons.

nodes_flat.title: 4.3. Policy Statement and Principles
nodes_flat.summary: We offer three nutritious meals daily: morning snack, lunch, and afternoon snack. Meals are whole, unprocessed, organic, seasonal, and locally sourced ("km 0"), free of fried products, low in salt, and without added sugars. We accommodate medical dietary needs (e.g., allergies/intolerances with allergist/gastroenterologist certificate) and personal preferences (vegetarian/vegan). A minimum of one vegetarian day per week is provided. Students learn "Practical Life" skills by participating in meal setup/cleanup, fostering community. Menus are reviewed monthly by the Imagine team and a nutritional specialist.
nodes_flat.content:
**Nutritional Balance:** We are committed to offering three nutritious meals daily: A portion snack in the morning, lunch, and snack in the afternoon, with a focus on whole, unprocessed foods. Our meals are free of fried products, low in salt, and free from added sugars.

**Sustainability and Environment:** In line with Montessori values and our commitment to environmental stewardship, ingredients used in school meals are organic, seasonal, and locally sourced (“km 0”). This not only supports the health of our students but also contributes to global sustainability efforts.

**Inclusive Diets:** We recognize and accommodate specific dietary needs. This includes strict adherence to medical requirements (e.g., gluten intolerance, allergies with a certificate from allergologists or gastroenterologists) and personal dietary preferences (e.g., vegetarian, vegan). A minimum of a day each week is dedicated to vegetarian meals for the entire school community.

**Cultural and Social Aspects of Eating:** Meal times are integral to the Montessori "Practical Life" curriculum. Students actively participate in setting the table, serving food, and cleaning up afterward, learning valuable skills such as responsibility, community, and respect for their environment. Meals are shared within classrooms to foster a sense of community, making food a social experience that is nutritionally and emotionally enriching.

**Preparation with Care:** Our meals are lovingly prepared by our cooks, who bring passion and dedication to cooking. Their goal is to nourish the body and mind of each student and staff member through wholesome, carefully prepared meals.

**Feedback Mechanisms**

We collect monthly feedback from the Imagine team to review the menu for the following month and address any areas for improvement. All our menus are reviewed by a nutritional specialist to ensure they meet the highest standards of health and well-being.

nodes_flat.title: 4.4. Considerations for Fasting for Religious Reasons
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori supports religious fasting. Families must submit a formal request to the Secretary, initiating a Phidias process. Fasting is generally for puberty age (12-15 years); younger children can do flexible, shorter, or partial fasting. Quiet spaces, like the science lab, free from food smells, are provided for reading or prayer, subject to logistical possibilities due to classroom-based dining. Staff and students should refrain from eating/drinking near fasters. Health is paramount: if a student is visibly unwell, at risk of dehydration, or requests food/water (e.g., due to menstruation, dizziness), the school will provide immediate care before contacting parents. Fasting students should avoid strenuous activity and excessive heat. PE can be modified to low-intensity activities or sitting out. Afternoon fatigue may require quiet rest. Fasting is a personal and family decision; the school does not monitor or enforce it.
nodes_flat.content:
Imagine Montessori School acknowledges and respects the cultural and religious diversity within our community. In alignment with our commitment to inclusivity and student well-being, the following measures are in place to support students fasting for religious reasons:

*   **Formal request** - Families who wish for their child to participate in fasting at school must submit a request to the Secretary. This request will initiate a Phidias process that must be reviewed and signed.
*   **Fasting Age Considerations** – While fasting is a personal and family decision, it is generally recommended for older children who can manage the physical and mental demands. Fasting during school hours is generally considered appropriate from puberty age, between 12 and 15 years old. Younger children who wish to participate should do so in a flexible manner, with shorter fasts or partial fasting, in consultation with their families.
*   **Provision of Quiet Spaces** – Fasting students will have access to a quiet space, free from food smells, such as the science lab, where they may read, engage in quiet conversation, or pray as needed. However, as Imagine Montessori School follows a classroom-based dining model without a common dining hall, the availability of such a space will depend on logistical possibilities within the school. Every effort will be made to provide a suitable environment that respects the needs of fasting students while maintaining the integrity of our Montessori approach to shared meal experiences.
*   **Respect for Fasting Students** – Staff and students are encouraged to be mindful of those fasting for religious reasons by refraining from eating or drinking in their presence.
*   **Health and Well-being Considerations** – While fasting is a personal and family decision, there may be circumstances in which a student requires food or water during the school day, such as feeling unwell, experiencing dizziness, due to menstruation or other health reasons.
    In cases where a student is visibly unwell or at risk of dehydration, the school reserves the right to provide immediate care, including offering water or food, if necessary, before contacting the parents. The well-being and safety of the child will always be the priority.
    Furthermore, if a fasting student requests food and/or water, it will be made available to them according to the school's standard procedures.
*   **Outdoor and Physical Activity Adjustments** – Fasting students may use outdoor spaces for socializing but should be mindful of their own energy levels and avoid excessive exposure to heat or strenuous activity. Parents are encouraged to have conversations with their children about these considerations to help them make informed choices regarding their activities while fasting.
*   **Afternoon Accommodations** – As fasting students may experience fatigue later in the day, if needed, we encourage them to find a quiet space to rest, read, or engage in low-energy activities as needed.
*   **Physical Education (PE) Modifications** – Participation in PE may be physically demanding while fasting. Students who require adjustments should be permitted to sit out or engage in alternative low-intensity activities, under the supervision of the same teacher.
*   **Respect for Personal and Family Decisions** – While the school provides accommodations for students who are fasting for religious reasons, it is considered a personal and family choice. The school does not monitor or enforce fasting, and students are responsible for managing their own participation in accordance with their family’s expectations.

nodes_flat.title: 4.5. Monitoring and Review
nodes_flat.summary: The Food Policy's effectiveness is monitored through regular feedback from students, parents, and staff. The "Biannual Review" process, conducted annually by the school leadership team, ensures continuous improvement in food provision, sustainability, and adherence to dietary requirements, with adjustments made as needed.
nodes_flat.content:
The effectiveness of this policy will be monitored through the regular feedback from students, parents, and staff.

Biannual Review: The Food Policy will be reviewed annually by the school leadership team and adjusted as needed to ensure continuous improvement in food provision, sustainability, and adherence to dietary requirements.

nodes_flat.title: 4.6. Reference Documents
nodes_flat.summary: School operations and health policies are guided by Valencian Community laws: Decreto 253/2019 (school organization), Ley 10/2014 (Art. 56) & Ley 1/1994 (promoting healthy habits, disease prevention in schools). Our food policy reflects Montessori principles like practical life skills, community, and environmental respect. Health, as per WHO, is defined as complete physical, mental, and social well-being.
nodes_flat.content:
*   Decreto 253/2019, de 29 de noviembre: Regulates the organisation and functioning of public schools providing Early Childhood and Primary Education in the Valencian Community.
*   Ley 10/2014, de 29 de diciembre, de Salud de la Comunitat Valenciana: Article 56 specifies the importance of promoting healthy habits and preventing diseases in the school environment.
*   Ley 1/1994, de 28 de marzo, de Salud Escolar: Establishes actions related to health in schools, with a focus on promoting health and preventing diseases.
*   Montessori Educational Principles: The food policy aligns with the principles of Montessori education, where practical life skills, community building, and respect for the environment are key aspects of the curriculum.
*   **World Health Organization Constitution**: (WHO): “Health is a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being, and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity.”

Contact dpd@imginemontessori.es for questions.

nodes_flat.title: 5.1. Scope
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori School (ZUBI EDUCATION, S.L.), as Data Controller for ZUBI GROUP IMPACT, S.L., prioritizes your personal data protection. We adhere to RGPD (EU 2016/679) and LOPDGDD (O.L. 3/2018). By providing your information or using our website, you acknowledge this Privacy Policy. It details who manages your data, why we collect and use it, your rights, and our privacy protection measures.
nodes_flat.content:
The protection of your personal data is very important to ZUBI EDUCATION, S.L. (Imagine Montessori School, hereinafter referred to as ‘The entity’), a company belonging to ZUBI GROUP IMPACT, S.L. as Data Controller, we inform you that, in accordance with the provisions of Regulation (EU) 2016/679, of 27 April, (RGPD) and O.L. 3/2018, of 5 December, on data protection and guarantee of digital rights (LOPDGDD), we will treat your data as reflected in this Privacy Policy.

By providing us with your personal information and using our website, we understand that you have read and understood the terms relating to the personal data protection information set out herein.

This notice will provide you with details of who the data controller is, the purposes for which your personal information will be processed, how we collect it, why we collect it, how we use it, your rights and the processes we have put in place to protect your privacy.

nodes_flat.title: 5.2. Terminology
nodes_flat.summary: What's 'Personal Data'? It's any info about you or your child – name, address, even banking. 'Processing' is how ZUBI EDUCATION, S.L. (Imagine Montessori School), our 'Data Controller', handles it: collecting, storing, using. Your 'Consent' is vital; it must be clear, and you can withdraw it anytime. We adhere to GDPR (EU law) and LOPDGDD (Spanish law). As a 'Data Subject', you have 'User Rights' to access, correct, or erase your data. 'Sensitive Data' (e.g., health) isn't collected unless legally required. Data won't go to 'Third Parties' or outside the EEA without strict safeguards. A 'DPO' oversees compliance to prevent 'Data Breaches'.
nodes_flat.content:
**Personal Data**: Any information relating to an identified or identifiable individual. This includes but is not limited to identifying information such as name, address, contact details, and any other data that may be collected through our services, such as social circumstances, employment status, or banking information.

**Processing**: Any operation or set of operations performed on personal data, such as collection, storage, organization, retrieval, use, dissemination, or destruction. This term refers to how your personal data is managed throughout its lifecycle.

**Data Controller**: The entity responsible for determining the purposes and means of processing personal data. In this case, the data controller is ZUBI EDUCATION, S.L. (Imagine Montessori School), part of ZUBI GROUP IMPACT, S.L.

**Consent**: A clear, informed, and explicit agreement from the individual to process their personal data. Consent can be given electronically or on paper, as specified in our data collection forms. Consent is required for any processing activity, and it can be withdrawn at any time.

**GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)**: The regulation (EU) 2016/679, which governs data protection and privacy across the European Union and aims to protect the privacy and personal data of individuals.

**LOPDGDD (Ley Orgánica 3/2018, de 5 de diciembre)**: The Spanish Organic Law on Data Protection and the Guarantee of Digital Rights, which complements the GDPR and governs data protection in Spain.

**Data Subject**: The individual whose personal data is being processed. In this policy, the data subject refers to individuals who provide their personal data to ZUBI EDUCATION, S.L., either as users or clients.

**Third Parties**: Any organization or individual outside of ZUBI EDUCATION, S.L. to whom personal data may be disclosed for processing, in compliance with legal obligations or contractual agreements.

**Data Protection Officer (DPO)**: The individual responsible for overseeing data protection activities within the entity. This includes ensuring compliance with data protection laws and addressing any concerns about personal data processing.

**Sensitive Data**: Personal data that is more sensitive in nature, such as data about race, ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, or health information. We do not collect sensitive data unless required by law.

**Data Breach**: Any unauthorized access to or disclosure of personal data, accidental or unlawful destruction, or loss of personal data. We implement strict security measures to prevent such incidents.

**User Rights**: The rights granted to individuals under data protection laws, including the right to access, rectify, erase, restrict, and object to the processing of their personal data. Users may also request data portability or withdraw consent for specific processing activities.

**International Data Transfer**: The movement of personal data outside the European Economic Area (EEA). Personal data will only be transferred to countries with adequate protection or under legal safeguards such as standard contractual clauses.

nodes_flat.title: 5.3. Policy Statement and Principles
nodes_flat.summary: We handle your data securely and transparently, following key principles. We only collect information for specific, legal reasons, such as your consent (GDPR Art. 6.1) or for a contract (Art. 6.2). We limit data collection to what's necessary (data minimisation) and use it only for stated purposes. Your data is kept accurate, secure with appropriate measures, and retained only as long as needed. Transfers outside the EU/EEA are protected. Sharing with third parties follows laws and contractual safeguards. We also comply with marketing and cookie legislation.
nodes_flat.content:
We have always been committed to delivering our services to the highest standards, which includes treating your data securely and transparently. Our principles are:

**Legality:** We will only collect your personal data for specified explicit and legitimate purposes. To comply with our legal and regulatory obligations, we rely on the following provisions:

*   Art. 6.1. GDPR. On the express consent you have given us for the processing of your data.
*   Art. 6.2 GDPR. For the performance of a contract to which you as a data subject are a party or for the implementation of contractual measures at your request.

**Data minimisation:** We limit the collection of personal data to what is strictly relevant and necessary for the purposes for which it was collected.

**Purpose limitation**: We will only collect your personal data for the stated purposes and only in accordance with your wishes.

**Accuracy**: We will keep your personal data accurate and up to date.

**Data Security:** We implement appropriate technical and organisational measures proportionate to the risks to ensure that your data is not subject to damage, such as unauthorised disclosure or access, accidental or unlawful destruction or accidental loss or alteration and any other form of unlawful processing.

**Retention:** We retain your personal data lawfully and appropriately and only for as long as is necessary for the purposes for which it was collected.

International transfers: Where your data is to be transferred outside the EU/EEA it will be adequately protected.

**Third parties**: Access and transfer of personal data to third parties is carried out in accordance with applicable laws and regulations and with appropriate contractual safeguards.

**Direct Marketing and Cookies:** We comply with applicable advertising and cookie legislation.

nodes_flat.title: 5.4. Ownership of the data and the purpose for which it is used
nodes_flat.summary: When you provide personal data via forms or interact with us, it enters our database. We *always* require your clear, active permission (express consent) to process this data. Online, you must tick checkboxes; without this, processing stops. For paper forms, a signature is needed. Express consent means a positive action, never silence. We record all consents. Data for website services also needs your explicit "yes" after we inform you. Contact forms are available for suggestions.
nodes_flat.content:
When you use the various electronic and paper registration forms in the entity you provide us with personal data that are incorporated into our database. You also generate information because of your interaction with us that we may retain. All data processing carried out by the entity is always and, in any case, based on your express consent.

To obtain consent, by electronic means, you must tick the checkboxes accompanying the data collection form. Without express authorisation, the data will not be processed, and the website will not allow the process to continue when processing is necessary. Our website generates a record of each of the consents given to be able to accredit their effective provision and date.

When we collect information from the user by another means, for example, a written form, we will obtain the user's consent to the processing by means of a signature on a paper document.

Express consent is understood to be that which implies a positive act on the part of the user, never basing processing on the mere silence or inaction of the user.

Likewise, the data you provide us with to access any service or functionality that we make available to you on the website will also be processed, always informing you of the processing that will be carried out and obtaining your express consent.

In addition, we make available to users of the website, forms to contact us, so you can send us your suggestions.

nodes_flat.title: 5.10. Data transfers outside the EEA
nodes_flat.summary: When your personal data moves from the European Economic Area (EEA) to non-EU countries, we ensure its protection. If the European Commission (EC) recognizes a non-EU State has 'adequate' data protection, transfers occur. For other non-EU States, we use a 'derogation' (exception) or implement security measures. These include EC-approved Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or, if applicable, Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs). You can request details of these measures via point 13.
nodes_flat.content:
In the case of international transfers originating in the European Economic Area (EEA) where the European Commission has recognised that a non-EU State has provided an adequate level of data protection, your personal data may be transferred on that basis.

For transfers to non-EU States whose level of protection has not been recognised by the European Commission, we will either use a derogation applicable to that situation or implement the following security measures to ensure the protection of your personal data:

*   Standard contractual clauses approved by the European Commission.
*   Binding Corporate Rules, where applicable.

To obtain a copy of these security measures or information about where they are available, you can send us a written request as set out in point 13 below.

nodes_flat.title: 5.11. How long will we retain your personal data?
nodes_flat.summary: We'll keep your personal data for the maximum period required by law or for operational needs like account maintenance, customer management, and responding to legal/regulatory requests. Most customer information is retained during and after our contractual relationship, as detailed in our cookie policy.
nodes_flat.content: We will retain your personal data for the maximum period required in order to comply with applicable laws and regulations or otherwise in connection with our operational requirements, such as proper account maintenance, facilitating customer management and responding to legal claims or regulatory requests. For example, most customer information is retained for the duration of the contractual relationship and after the termination of the contractual relationship, as detailed in our cookie policy on the company's websites.

nodes_flat.title: 5.12. What are your rights and how can you exercise them?
nodes_flat.summary: You have rights over your personal data: Access, Rectification, Erasure, Restriction, Opposition (absolute for direct marketing), and Portability. You can also withdraw consent anytime. To exercise these, send a letter to C/ Meliana, 5, 46019 Valencia or email dpd@imginemontessori.es, attaching your NIF/ID for verification. If no response within one month, you can lodge a complaint with the Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) at Calle Jorge Juan, 6, 28001 Madrid.
nodes_flat.content:
In accordance with the applicable regulation, you have the following rights:

*   **Access**: you can obtain information regarding the processing of your personal data and a copy of it.
*   **Rectification**: if you consider your personal data to be incorrect or incomplete, you may request that such data be amended accordingly.
*   **Erasure**: you may request the erasure of your personal data to the extent permitted by law.
*   **Restriction**: you may request the restriction of the processing of your personal data.
*   **Opposition**: you can object to the processing of your data considering your particular situation. You have an absolute right to object to the processing of your personal data for direct marketing purposes, which includes profiling related to direct marketing.
*   **Portability of your data**: where applicable, you have the right to have your data returned to you or, where possible, transferred to a third party.

Where you have given your consent for a specific purpose, you have the right to withdraw your consent at any time, without affecting the lawfulness of the processing.

If you wish to exercise the aforementioned rights, please send a letter or e-mail to the following addresses: C/ Meliana, 5, CP 46019 Valencia, or to the email dpd@imginemontessori.es including a copy or scanned copy of your NIF or supporting document in order to verify your identity.

In accordance with the applicable legislation, in addition to the aforementioned rights, you have the right to lodge a complaint with the competent supervisory authority, the Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD), located at Calle Jorge Juan, 6 CP 28001 Madrid, if we have not previously responded to your request to exercise your rights within a period of one month.
